children.name: Erinaceus
children.description: True hedgehogs of Europe and Asia — iconic, spiny insectivores that have inhabited the Old World for around 15 million years. Their 5,000–7,000 hollow keratin spines are modified hairs, and when threatened a hedgehog can roll into a near-perfect ball by contracting the orbicularis muscle that runs around its back, protecting all exposed soft tissue. Hedgehogs are partial hibernators, entering torpor when temperatures drop and food becomes scarce. The Western European hedgehog has declined by roughly 50% in the UK since 2000, driven by agricultural intensification, habitat fragmentation, road mortality, and badger predation.

children.name: Atelerix
children.description: African hedgehogs — four-toed hedgehogs of sub-Saharan Africa and the Sahara. The African pygmy hedgehog (Atelerix albiventris) has become a popular pet worldwide, particularly in North America and Japan, though ownership is restricted in some US states. African hedgehogs tend to be smaller and more arid-adapted than their European relatives; some can aestivate during dry season in addition to hibernating in cold seasons. The four-toed hedgehog lacks the first toe on its hind feet, distinguishing it from European species.

children.name: Hemiechinus
children.description: Long-eared hedgehogs — a genus notable for their large, prominent ears that aid thermoregulation in hot desert environments. The ears radiate heat and provide acute hearing for detecting prey underground. These hedgehogs are faster runners than Erinaceus species and rely less on rolling into a ball, preferring to flee or jab at threats. They inhabit arid and semi-arid regions across Central Asia, South Asia, and North Africa.

children.name: Paraechinus
children.description: Desert hedgehogs — a genus adapted to some of the harshest arid environments on Earth, from the Sahara to the Thar Desert. Members have a characteristic dark mask or facial pattern and a bald patch on the crown of the head. They are highly insectivorous but also take scorpions and small reptiles. Their metabolic adaptations allow them to survive long periods without water, obtaining moisture from prey.

children.name: Mesechinus
children.description: Steppe hedgehogs — a small genus of hedgehogs native to East Asia, occupying the transition zone between temperate forest and the Mongolian steppe. They were formerly placed within Erinaceus but are now recognised as a distinct genus on genetic grounds. Distinguished by shorter ears and a more robust build compared to Hemiechinus, with which they share parts of their range.

children.name: Echinosorex
children.description: The greater moonrat — a single living species representing one of the most primitive members of the hedgehog family. Despite the common name, it is neither a rat nor a true mole but a large, naked-tailed insectivore with a distinct pungent ammonia-like odour used for communication and defence. Moonrats lack spines entirely, having instead coarse, bristly fur. They are the largest living erinaceids, reaching 1.4 kg.

children.name: Podogymnura
children.description: Philippine gymnures — spiny-less erinaceids endemic to the Philippines. These small, shrew-like animals inhabit montane tropical forests. Like the greater moonrat, they lack spines and rely on scent glands for protection. The two known species are restricted to Mindanao and adjacent islands, representing a relict lineage that survived in isolated mountain refugia.

children.name: Podogymnura truei
children.description: Podogymnura truei, also known as the Mindanao gymnure, Mindanao moonrat, or Mindanao wood shrew, is a mammal of the family Erinaceidae.

children.name: Hylomys
children.description: Moonrats and gymnures — the most primitive members of the hedgehog family, lacking spines entirely. Moonrats superficially resemble large shrews or small opossums and represent the closest living relatives of true hedgehogs. The greater moonrat (Echinosorex gymnura) is the largest insectivore in Southeast Asia, reaching 40 cm in body length and smelling powerfully of ammonia. Gymnures are forest floor specialists of Southeast Asian rainforest; the lesser gymnure (Hylomys suillus) is one of the most abundant small mammals in the cloud forests of Borneo.

children.name: Hemiechinus
children.description: Long-eared hedgehogs of Central and South Asia and the Middle East, distinguished by conspicuously large ears that aid heat dissipation in arid environments. They run faster than true hedgehogs and are less inclined to roll into a defensive ball, preferring to flee. Capable of short bursts of speed and are more agile climbers than European hedgehogs.

children.name: Mesechinus
children.description: Steppe and montane hedgehogs of Central and East Asia, sometimes called Daurian hedgehogs. Adapted to cold, open grassland and steppe habitats from Mongolia to northeastern China. Smaller than Erinaceus but with similar habits; hibernate deeply in winter in burrows dug into stiff soil.

children.name: Paraechinus
children.description: Desert hedgehogs of North Africa, Arabia, and South Asia — the most arid-adapted members of the family. Capable of surviving in true desert conditions by aestivating during extreme heat and drought. Their larger ears and paler colouration reflect their desert lifestyle. Often eat scorpions and other prey that other hedgehogs avoid.

children.name: Echinosorex
children.description: Monotypic genus containing the greater moonrat, the largest insectivore in Asia and one of the most distinctive members of the hedgehog family. Unlike its relatives it bears no spines, instead covered in coarse black and white fur. Produces an intensely musky odour resembling ammonia, used for territorial marking along watercourses.

children.name: Neotetracus
children.description: Monotypic genus of primitive insectivores from southern China and adjacent Southeast Asia. The shrew gymnure superficially resembles a large shrew with its elongated snout and slender build, but belongs firmly within Erinaceidae. Represents one of the oldest lineages within the family.
